137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

12	## Question
13	
14	For the seven scaffolds of the P3b "injective `O_X`-modules are Čech-acyclic"
15	bridge (`cechFreePresheafComplex`, `cechComplex_hom_identification`,
16	`cechFreeComplex_quasiIso`, `presheafModules_enoughInjectives`,
17	`cech_delta_functor_presheaves`, `injective_cech_acyclic`, plus the P3 re-sign of
18	`CechAcyclic.affine`): what is Mathlib's idiom (existing API to reuse, or the
19	construction path if absent), does the proposed name/shape risk a parallel API,
20	and what is the cost of misalignment?
21	
22	## Project artifact(s)
23	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean` — the whole P3b block
24	  is *about to be scaffolded* (nothing shipped yet). Existing anchors:
25	  `CechComplex` (`:737`, relative complex in `S.Modules`), `CechAcyclic.affine`
26	  (`:764`, the P3 sorry, NOT protected), `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`
27	  (`:801`, PROTECTED).

29	  machinery" (`def:cech_free_presheaf_complex`, `lem:cech_complex_hom_identification`,
30	  `lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso`, `lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives`,
31	  `lem:cech_delta_functor_presheaves`, `lem:injective_cech_acyclic`).
32	
33	## Mathlib ground truth (verified via LSP this iter)
34	
35	The project's sheaf side is `X.Modules = SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf`
36	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`). The presheaf side already exists:
37	
38	- **`PresheafOfModules R`** for `R : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat` (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf`).
39	  `Abelian` (`PresheafOfModules.instAbelian`) and `Preadditive` instances present.
40	  For a scheme there is the abbreviation **`X.PresheafOfModules`**
41	  (`= PresheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf.val`, site `(Opens ↥X)ᵒᵖ`).
42	- **`Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X : X.Modules ⥤ X.PresheafOfModules`** — the
43	  `Mod ↪ PMod` inclusion, **a right adjoint**
44	  (`...instIsRightAdjointPresheafOfModulesToPresheafOfModules`).
45	- **`PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (α : R₀ ⟶ R.val)`**
46	  (`...Presheaf.Sheafification`): `sheafification α ⊣ (forget R) ⋙ restrictScalars α`.
47	  For a scheme `α = 𝟙` and this is the `sheafification ⊣ inclusion` pair.
48	- **`PresheafOfModules.free R : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u) ⥤ PresheafOfModules R`** with
49	  **`PresheafOfModules.freeAdjunction R : free R ⊣ toPresheaf R ⋙ whiskeringRight … (forget Ab)`**
50	  (`...Presheaf.Free`). `freeObjDesc`/`freeHomEquiv` give the hom-set bijection.
51	- **`PresheafOfModules.evaluation R X : PresheafOfModules R ⥤ ModuleCat (R.obj X)`** —
52	  the exact "sections over `X`" functor.
53	- **`CategoryTheory.Injective.injective_of_adjoint (adj : L ⊣ R) (J) [Injective J]
54	  [L.PreservesMonomorphisms] : Injective (R.obj J)`**
55	  (`...Preadditive.Injective.Basic`); plus
56	  `Functor.preservesInjectiveObjects_of_adjunction_of_preservesMonomorphisms`
57	  (`...Injective.Preserves`) and `Adjunction.map_injective`.
58	- **`IsGrothendieckAbelian.enoughInjectives`** (instance) and
59	  **`instIsGrothendieckAbelianModuleCat`** — present, as the directive said.
60	- **`Functor.rightDerived`** (`...Abelian.RightDerived`) needs
61	  `[HasInjectiveResolutions C]` + `F.Additive`; `rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf` identifies
62	  `R⁰F ≅ F` for left-exact `F`. Universal property machinery:

65	**Confirmed ABSENT (all `#synth`-failed in the project's universes):**

74	## Decisions identified
75	
76	### Decision 1: `cechFreePresheafComplex` — category + the `j_!` shriek
77	
78	- **Mathlib idiom**: the category is **`X.PresheafOfModules`** (no parallel needed).
79	  For the shriek: Mathlib has **no** `j_!` extension-by-zero for presheaves of
80	  modules, BUT it has the canonical representing-object machine
81	  **`PresheafOfModules.free`**. The blueprint summand
82	  `(j_{i_0…i_p})_! (O_X|_{U_{i_0…i_p}})` is, up to canonical iso, exactly
83	  **`(PresheafOfModules.free R).obj (yoneda.obj U_{i_0…i_p})`**: on `V` it is the
84	  free `R(V)`-module on `Hom(V,U)` = `R(V)` if `V ⊆ U`, else `0` — precisely
85	  extension-by-zero of `O_X|_U`. Its defining property
86	  `Hom(free(yoneda U), F) ≅ F(U)` is `freeAdjunction` + Yoneda. `yoneda` on
87	  `Opens ↥X` is verified to typecheck (`Opens ↥X ⥤ (Opens ↥X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`).
88	- **Project's proposed path**: a definition named `cechFreePresheafComplex` building
89	  `⨁ (j_…)_! O|_{U_…}` "with the alternating Čech differential". The blueprint prose
90	  introduces a bespoke `j_!` ("the shriek functor … `(j_!G)(W)=G(W)` if `W⊆U` else 0").
91	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost **iff** a bespoke `j_!`/extension-by-zero functor is
92	  authored. ALIGNED iff `j_!(O|_U)` is realized as `free(yoneda U)`.
93	- **Cost of divergence**: a hand-rolled `j_!` is a parallel API to `free`. It would
94	  need its own adjunction-with-restriction lemma to recover
95	  `Hom(j_! O|_U, F) = F(U)` (decision 2) — duplicating `freeAdjunction` — and its own
96	  functoriality for the index-dropping maps. Using `free` gives both for free: the
97	  differentials are `free.map` of the representable index-dropping maps
98	  `yoneda(U_{i_0…i_{p+1}}) → yoneda(U_{i_0…î_j…})` (inclusion of opens), and the
99	  Yoneda identity is one adjunction iso.
100	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on the building blocks (`X.PresheafOfModules`,
101	  `free`, `yoneda`, `evaluation`); NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL for the assembled complex
102	  object (no Čech-of-presheaf-modules complex exists). Build it as a
103	  `ChainComplex X.PresheafOfModules ℕ` (degree `p` = `⨁_{I^{p+1}} free(yoneda U_…)`,
104	  homological/chain variance per the blueprint's `K(𝒰)_•`). Do NOT introduce a
105	  named `j_!`.
106	
107	### Decision 2: `cechComplex_hom_identification` — `Hom(K_•, F) = Č•(𝒰,F)`
108	
109	- **Mathlib idiom**: `(freeAdjunction R).homEquiv (yoneda.obj U) F` composed with the
110	  Yoneda section iso (`yonedaEquiv` / representable sections) gives
111	  `Hom_{PMod}(free(yoneda U), F) ≅ F(U)` naturally in `F`. The "shriek–restriction
112	  adjunction termwise" the directive asks for **is** `freeAdjunction` (the left
113	  adjoint of "underlying presheaf of types"), not a separate `j_! ⊣ restriction`.
114	  Take the product/`⨁`-`Hom` over multi-indices and match differentials.
115	- **Project's proposed path**: `cechComplex_hom_identification : Hom(K_•,F) = Č•(𝒰,F)`.
116	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost on one point — **which `Č•`**. The project already has
117	  `CechComplex` (`:737`) = the **relative** complex `∏ (f|_{U_…})_* (F|_{U_…})` in
118	  `S.Modules` (pushforward along `f`). The blueprint's `Č•(𝒰,F)` here is the
119	  **sections** complex `∏ F(U_{i_0…i_p})` of `O_X(U)`-modules — a *different, simpler*
120	  object. `Hom(K_•, F)` lands in `O_X(U)`-modules, not `S.Modules`.
121	- **Cost of divergence**: if `cechComplex_hom_identification` is stated against the
122	  relative `CechComplex`, the types won't line up and a bridge between "section Čech
123	  complex" and "relative pushforward Čech complex" gets invented — a parallel `Č•`.
124	  Recommendation: introduce the **presheaf section Čech complex** `Č•(𝒰,F)` as its own
125	  `CochainComplex (ModuleCat (O_X U)) ℕ` (or as `(evaluation _ (op U)).mapHomologicalComplex`
126	  applied to nothing — it is literally the `Hom`-complex), and reconcile with the
127	  relative `CechComplex` only where actually needed downstream.
128	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (use `freeAdjunction` + Yoneda; reuse `evaluation`),
129	  with a FLAG to keep the section-`Č•` and the relative-`CechComplex` distinct rather
130	  than forking a third notion.
131	
132	### Decision 3: `cechFreeComplex_quasiIso` — `K_•` resolves `O_𝒰[0]`
133	
134	- **Mathlib idiom**: none specific. Exactness of a complex of presheaves is checked
135	  objectwise (`PresheafOfModules` (co)limits and homology are objectwise); the
136	  sectionwise extended complex over each `W` is contractible by the explicit homotopy
137	  `h(s)_{i_0…} = (i_0=i_fix) s_{i_1…}`. Mathlib has `Homotopy`/`HomotopyEquiv` and
138	  `Preadditive`/homology API but not this Čech contractibility. (`ExtraDegeneracy`
139	  exists but is simplicial/chain and has the same global-`i_fix` mismatch noted in
140	  `p3-localisation.md`; do not route through it.)
141	- **Gap**: divergent-and-necessary (no idiom).
142	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL. Build the sectionwise contracting homotopy.
143	
144	### Decision 4: `presheafModules_enoughInjectives`
145	
146	- **Mathlib idiom**: the intended `IsGrothendieckAbelian.enoughInjectives` engine is
147	  real, BUT the prerequisite `IsGrothendieckAbelian (PresheafOfModules R)` is **absent**,
148	  and there is **no functor-category transfer** (`IsGrothendieckAbelian (C ⥤ D)` for
149	  `D` Grothendieck is also absent), and **`AB5 (PresheafOfModules R)` is absent**. Only
150	  `HasColimits`/`HasFilteredColimits` are present. So the cheap "instance already
151	  there" hope is false.
152	- **Project's proposed path**: derive enough-injectives from Grothendieck-abelian.
153	- **Gap**: divergent-and-necessary, **and expensive**.
154	- **Cost**: the cheapest real path is `IsGrothendieckAbelian.mk` supplying (i) `AB5`
155	  (objectwise from `ModuleCat`'s AB5 — needs an objectwise-filtered-colimit-exactness
156	  argument), (ii) a separating family (the `free(yoneda U)` objects separate), plus
157	  LocallySmall. That is a multi-lemma build. **KEY STRATEGIC NOTE:** this lemma is
158	  **NOT on the critical path to `injective_cech_acyclic`** (decision 6) — see below —
159	  so the project should weigh building it at all.
160	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (high cost; reconsider necessity).
161	
162	### Decision 5: `cech_delta_functor_presheaves`
163	
164	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Functor.rightDerived` exists but **requires
165	  `HasInjectiveResolutions (PresheafOfModules R)`**, i.e. it *depends on decision 4*.
166	  The universal-δ-functor/effaceability identification (`Ȟ^p ≅ R^p Ȟ^0`) is NOT a
167	  packaged Mathlib theorem in usable form: `IsRightDerivedFunctor` is
168	  localization-flavored and matching Čech cohomology to it is substantial. Mathlib's
169	  cohomological-δ-functor universality API is thin.
170	- **Gap**: divergent-and-necessary, expensive, and **chained behind decision 4**.
171	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (high cost; also not needed for decision 6).
172	
173	### Decision 6: `injective_cech_acyclic`
174	
175	- **Mathlib idiom (two independent parts):**
176	  1. *injective sheaf ⟹ injective presheaf*: ALIGNED — apply
177	     **`Injective.injective_of_adjoint`** to `sheafificationAdjunction` (left adjoint
178	     `sheafification`, right adjoint = the inclusion `toPresheafOfModules`), with
179	     `sheafification.PreservesMonomorphisms` (it is exact). Exactly the Stacks
180	     "right adjoint of an exact left adjoint preserves injectives".
181	  2. *the vanishing itself*: it follows **directly** from decisions 2+3 + injectivity,
182	     WITHOUT decisions 4 or 5. `K_• → O_𝒰` is an exact augmented complex (decision 3);
183	     for injective `I`, `Hom(-,I)` is exact, so `Hom(K_•,I) = Č•(𝒰,I)` (decision 2) is
184	     exact in positive degrees. This is the Stacks proof of `lemma-injective-trivial-cech`
185	     and it does **not** invoke the δ-functor identification.
186	- **Gap**: ALIGNED on part 1; part 2 is light gap-fill once 2+3 exist.
187	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on the injective-transfer; the rest is gap-fill that
188	  bypasses decisions 4–5.
189	
190	### Decision 7: P3 re-sign of `CechAcyclic.affine`
191	
192	- **Mathlib idiom**: locked in `analogies/p3-localisation.md`. Re-sign to carry
193	  `(s : ι → Γ(X,O_X)) (hs : Ideal.span (Set.range s) = ⊤)` and obtain the cover via
194	  `(Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop s hs).openCover : X.OpenCover`, fed into the
195	  (general-`OpenCover`) `CechComplex`. The same `(s,hs)` bundle drives
196	  `exact_of_isLocalized_span`. The protected `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` and
197	  `CechComplex` keep their general signatures.
198	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (confirmed; idiomatic feed is `.openCover`).
199	
200	## Recommendation
201	
202	Build the P3b block on Mathlib's **existing** presheaf-of-modules API, not a parallel
203	one. (1) Category = `X.PresheafOfModules`. (2) The blueprint's `j_!(O_X|_U)` must be
204	`(PresheafOfModules.free _).obj (yoneda.obj U)` — do **not** author a bespoke
205	extension-by-zero functor; that is the single highest parallel-API risk, because it
206	would duplicate `freeAdjunction` (the very thing decision 2 needs) and `free`'s
207	functoriality. (3) `cechComplex_hom_identification` = `freeAdjunction.homEquiv` +
208	Yoneda + `evaluation`; state it against a *new presheaf section* `Č•(𝒰,F)` of
209	`O_X(U)`-modules, kept distinct from the existing relative `CechComplex`. (4)
210	`injective_cech_acyclic` should be routed through `Injective.injective_of_adjoint`
211	(`sheafificationAdjunction`) + the direct "exact `Hom(-,I)` kills positive Čech
212	cohomology" argument, which needs only the free-complex resolution (decisions 2–3) and
213	**bypasses `presheafModules_enoughInjectives` (4) and `cech_delta_functor_presheaves`
214	(5)**. Decisions 4 and 5 are genuine Mathlib gaps with real cost (no Grothendieck
215	instance for presheaves of modules, no functor-category transfer, no AB5, and
216	`rightDerived` is gated on enough-injectives); since the headline result (6) does not
217	need them, treat 4–5 as optional and deprioritize them. Decision 7 is the already-locked
218	P3 re-sign. Risk order: **1 (bespoke `j_!`) > 2 (forked `Č•`) > 4,5 (expensive,
219	possibly-unnecessary gaps) > 3,6 (aligned gap-fill) > 7 (confirmed)**.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116

147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
20	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
21	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
22	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
23	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
24	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
25	
26	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
27	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
28	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
29	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
30	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
31	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
32	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
33	\[
34	  \begin{array}{ccc}
35	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
36	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
37	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
38	  \end{array}
39	\]
40	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
41	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
42	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
43	
44	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
45	
46	\begin{definition}
47	
48	\leanok
49	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}

162	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
163	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
164	
165	It is convenient to factor the construction of
166	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
167	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
168	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
169	
170	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
171	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
172	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
173	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
174	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
175	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
176	\[
177	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
178	    \;=\;
179	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
180	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
181	\]
182	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
183	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
184	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
185	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
186	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
187	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
188	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
189	
190	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
191	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
192	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
193	\[
194	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
195	  \qquad
196	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
197	\]
198	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
199	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
200	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
201	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
202	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
203	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
204	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
205	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
206	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
207	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
208	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
209	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
210	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
211	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
212	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
213	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
214	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
215	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
216	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
217	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
218	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
219	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
220	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
221	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
222	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
223	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
224	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
225	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
226	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
227	
228	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
229	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
230	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
231	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
232	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
233	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
234	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
235	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
236	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
237	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
238	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
239	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
240	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
241	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
242	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
243	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
244	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
245	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
246	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
247	
248	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
249	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
250	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
251	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
252	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
253	
254	\begin{definition}\leanok
255	[Arrow of an affine cover]
256	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
258	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
259	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
260	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
261	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
262	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
263	  apply.
264	\end{definition}

272	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
273	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
274	  \[
275	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
276	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
277	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
278	  \]
279	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
280	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the

282	\end{definition}

286	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
288	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
289	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
290	  \[
291	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},

296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull comparison map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
300	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
301	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
302	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
303	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
304	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
305	  \[
306	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
307	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
308	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
310	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \;=\;
312	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
314	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
315	  \]
316	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
317	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
318	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
319	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
320	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
321	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
322	\end{definition}

324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
326	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
328	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
329	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
330	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
331	\end{lemma}
332	
333	\begin{proof}
334	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
335	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
336	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
337	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
338	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
339	\end{proof}

341	\begin{lemma}
342	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
343	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
345	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
346	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
347	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
348	  \[
349	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
350	  \]
351	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
352	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
353	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
354	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
355	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
356	\end{lemma}
357	
358	\begin{proof}
359	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
361	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
362	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
363	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
364	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
365	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
366	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
367	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
368	\end{proof}

370	\begin{lemma}
371	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
372	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
374	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
375	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
377	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
378	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
379	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
380	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
381	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
382	  \[
383	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
384	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
385	    \;=\;
386	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
387	  \]
388	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
389	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
390	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
391	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
396	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
397	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
398	\end{proof}

401	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
402	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
403	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}

405	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the

407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
408	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
409	  collapses to the transport-light form
410	  \[
411	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
412	      \;\circ\; e_2
413	    \;=\;
414	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
415	      \;\circ\; e_3,
416	  \]
417	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by

419	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
420	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
421	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
422	  composition (pentagon) law.
423	\end{lemma}

427	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
428	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
429	\end{proof}

503	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.

612	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
613	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
614	
615	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
616	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
617	affine cover: it compares the explicit {\v C}ech complex to the
618	injective-resolution-defined right-derived functors on the abelian category
619	\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
620	comparison rests on three presheaf-level facts, recorded here: a free complex
621	\(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves that \emph{represents} the {\v C}ech
622	functor (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} and
623	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}); the resolution property of that
624	complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}); and the resulting
625	identification of {\v C}ech cohomology with a right-derived
626	\(\delta\)-functor (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_delta_functor_presheaves}), which needs
627	\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) to have enough injectives
628	(Lemma~\ref{lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}).

826	% Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
827	% if $i \in I_1$.
828	% ...
829	% If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
830	% $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$
831	% ...
832	% Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
833	% $h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)$
834	% by the rule
835	% $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} = (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_p}$

839	  Exactness of a complex of presheaves is checked sectionwise, so it suffices to
840	  show that for every open \(W \subset X\) the extended complex of
841	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-modules
842	  \[
843	    \cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(W) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(W) \to
844	      \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) \to 0
845	  \]
846	  is acyclic; in fact it is contractible. Split the index set as \(I = I_1 \amalg
847	  I_2\), where \(i \in I_1\) iff \(W \subset U_i\). If \(I_1 = \varnothing\) the
848	  extended complex is identically \(0\). If \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) then
849	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) and, by the explicit
850	  description of the shriek presheaves, \(K(\mathcal{U})_p(W) =
851	  \bigoplus_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) with the alternating-sum
852	  differential. Fixing an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\), the prescription
853	  \[
854	    h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} =
855	      \begin{cases}
856	        s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}} & i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}, \\
857	        0 & i_0 \neq i_{\mathrm{fix}}
858	      \end{cases}
859	  \]
860	  defines a contracting homotopy: a direct computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\),
861	  so the identity of \(K^{\mathrm{ext}}_\bullet(W)\) is homotopic to zero. Hence the
862	  extended complex is exact over every \(W\), which is the claimed vanishing of the
863	  homology presheaves.
864	\end{proof}

The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
affine cover: it compares the explicit {\v C}ech complex to the
injective-resolution-defined right-derived functors on the abelian category
\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
comparison rests on three presheaf-level facts, recorded here: a free complex
\(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves that \emph{represents} the {\v C}ech
functor (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} and
Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}); the resolution property of that
complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}); and the resulting
identification of {\v C}ech cohomology with a right-derived
\(\delta\)-functor (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_delta_functor_presheaves}), which needs
\(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) to have enough injectives
(Lemma~\ref{lem:presheaf_modules_enough_injectives}).

The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
\(\delta\)-functor identification.

\textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
  the open immersion of each finite intersection. Write \((j)_{!}\) for the
  \emph{extension by zero} (the ``shriek'') functor on presheaves of modules along
  an open immersion \(j\): for a presheaf \(\mathcal{G}\) of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X|_U\)-modules, \((j_{!}\mathcal{G})(W) = \mathcal{G}(W)\) if
  \(W \subset U\) and \(0\) otherwise; it is the left adjoint of restriction. The
  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose term
  in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
  \[
    K(\mathcal{U})_p
      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},
  \]
  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the canonical maps
  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}} \to
  (j_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots
  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a
  \emph{projective} object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0
  \ldots i_p}}, -) = (-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is the exact ``sections over
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor; this is the structural feature exploited below.
\end{definition}

  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)

  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
  \]
  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
  \[
    K(\mathcal{U})_p
      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
  \]
  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Section {\v C}ech complex of a presheaf of modules]
  \label{def:section_cech_complex}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplex}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves,
  % definition of $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
  % $$
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
  % =
  % \prod\nolimits_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p + 1}}
  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
  % $$
  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
  % $$
  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
  % \longrightarrow
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
  % $$
  % by the formula
  % $$
  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
  % =
  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^{p + 1}
  % (-1)^j
  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
  % $$
  % It is straightforward to see that $d \circ d = 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-modules whose term in
  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
  \[
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
  \]
  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
  \[
    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
  \]
  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-modules.
  The two must not be conflated: the presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs
  entirely on this section complex.
\end{definition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cech_complex}
  The adjunction between the shriek functor \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\) and
  restriction gives, for each multi-index, the natural identification
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},\, \mathcal{F}
    \bigr)
    = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}}\bigl(
      \mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}},\, \mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
    \bigr)
    = \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}).
  \]
  Taking the product over multi-indices, \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p,
  \mathcal{F}) = \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
  identification the \((-1)^j\) index-dropping maps defining the differential of
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become exactly the alternating-sum restriction
  differential of \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the
  two cochain complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from
  the naturality of the adjunction.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
  \[
    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
    \bigr)
    \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
    \;\cong\;
    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
  \]
  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
  The argument has two independent parts.

  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
  left adjoint to the inclusion
  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).

  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\). Hence
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
\end{proof}
